text: Up to 45% of patients with Achilles or patellar tendinopathy may not respond to isolated eccentric loading programmes.

Eccentric-concentric loading (such as Silbernagel combined protocol for Achilles and heavy-slow resistance for patellar tendinopathy) has equivalent or superior evidence compared to isolated eccentric loading for clinical outcomes.

Improved neuromuscular performance (torque, work, endurance) is the only potential mechanism consistently associated with improved clinical outcomes in both Achilles and patellar tendon rehabilitation.

Heavy-slow resistance (HSR) loading for patellar tendinopathy is associated with reduced Doppler area, reduced anteroposterior diameter, and greater evidence of collagen turnover, suggesting actual tendon structural adaptation.

Improved imaging outcomes (reduced tendon diameter, reduced Doppler signal) are not consistently associated with clinical improvement in Achilles tendinopathy, including in all high-quality studies reviewed.
Clinicians should consider eccentric-concentric loading alongside or instead of isolated eccentric loading for both Achilles and patellar tendinopathy rehabilitation.
Improved jump performance is associated with clinical outcomes in Achilles tendinopathy but not patellar tendinopathy, suggesting mechanisms of recovery may differ between tendons.
